startScreenOptions.title: AN2.1-AN2.6 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 5 embedded questions for AN2.1-AN2.6.

interactions.label: Which of the following correctly matches a region of a long bone with its struct

params.question: Which of the following correctly matches a region of a long bone with its structural feature?

answers.text: Diaphysis — packed with spongy bone and covered by hyaline cartilage

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The diaphysis is the tubular shaft made of compact bone with a hollow medullary cavity. Spongy bone and a hyaline cartilage articular surface are features of the epiphysis.

answers.text: Epiphysis — contains the medullary cavity surrounded by compact bone

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The epiphysis is packed with spongy (cancellous) bone for load distribution. The medullary cavity and its compact bone wall are in the diaphysis, not the ends.

answers.text: Metaphysis — houses the growth plate in children, which fuses to form the epiphyseal line in adults

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The metaphysis is the transition zone between diaphysis and epiphysis. In children it contains the epiphyseal plate — the cartilage layer driving longitudinal growth — which fuses into the epiphyseal line in adults.

answers.text: Diaphysis — contains the growth plate where the bone actively lengthens

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The diaphysis is the shaft of the bone. Longitudinal growth occurs at the growth plate, which is located in the metaphysis — the transition zone between shaft and end.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the three regions: the diaphysis is the compact-bone shaft with the medullary cavity, the epiphysis is the spongy-bone end with a hyaline cartilage surface, and the metaphysis is the transition zone housing the growth plate.
Excellent. Understanding these three regions — diaphysis, epiphysis, and metaphysis — is essential for interpreting fractures, growth disorders, and joint pathology.

metadata.title: Which of the following correctly matches

interactions.label: How does intramembranous ossification differ from endochondral ossification?

params.question: How does intramembranous ossification differ from endochondral ossification?

answers.text: It occurs only in the long bones of the limbs

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Long bones of the limbs form through endochondral ossification. Intramembranous ossification produces the flat bones of the skull and the clavicle.

answers.text: It forms bone directly from mesenchyme without a cartilage stage

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. In intramembranous ossification, osteoblasts differentiate directly within mesenchyme (embryonic connective tissue), bypassing the cartilage model entirely. The flat bones of the skull and the clavicle form this way.

answers.text: It requires a pre-existing cartilage model that is gradually replaced by bone

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A cartilage model replaced by bone describes endochondral ossification. In intramembranous ossification, there is no cartilage precursor at all.

answers.text: It begins at secondary ossification centres in the epiphyses after birth

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Secondary centres appearing in the epiphyses is a feature of endochondral ossification. Intramembranous ossification does not follow the diaphysis–epiphysis pattern.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember: endochondral ossification replaces a cartilage model with bone (most bones), while intramembranous ossification forms bone directly from mesenchyme (skull and clavicle). The clavicle is the only long bone formed intramembranously.
Well done. The two pathways — endochondral (cartilage replaced by bone, most bones) and intramembranous (mesenchyme to bone, skull and clavicle) — are a key AN2.2 distinction.

interactions.label: On an X-ray, how can a sesamoid bone be distinguished from a fracture fragment?

params.question: On an X-ray, how can a sesamoid bone be distinguished from a fracture fragment?

answers.text: Sesamoid bones are found only adjacent to the knee joint

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Sesamoid bones occur at several sites — the patella at the knee, the pisiform at the wrist, and paired sesamoids beneath the first metatarsal. Location alone does not distinguish them from fragments.

answers.text: Fracture fragments have smooth, rounded margins with intact cortex

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This describes sesamoid bones, not fracture fragments. Fracture fragments have sharp, irregular edges with cortical disruption — the opposite pattern.

answers.text: Sesamoid bones are radio-lucent and invisible on plain radiographs

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Sesamoid bones are bony structures and are radio-opaque — clearly visible on plain X-rays, which is exactly why they can be confused with fracture fragments.

answers.text: Sesamoid bones have smooth, rounded edges with a complete cortex

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. A sesamoid has a smooth, well-defined cortical margin all around. Fracture fragments display sharp, jagged, irregular edges. This cortical-margin sign is a classic radiological differentiator.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember: sesamoid bones have smooth, rounded edges with an intact cortex. Fracture fragments have sharp, irregular margins. This cortical-margin sign is the key differentiator.
Good. This sesamoid-versus-fragment distinction is a practical radiological skill you will use when reading extremity X-rays.

interactions.label: All symphyses in the body, such as the pubic symphysis and intervertebral discs,

params.question: All symphyses in the body, such as the pubic symphysis and intervertebral discs, are located in the midline.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This is actually true — all symphyses lie in the midline of the body, with the pubic symphysis and intervertebral discs as key examples. If a fibrocartilage joint is not midline, it is not a symphysis.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Every symphysis — a secondary cartilaginous joint with a fibrocartilage disc — is a midline structure. The pubic symphysis and intervertebral discs are the classic examples, and this midline-only rule reliably distinguishes them from other joint types.

interactions.label: A child with hip disease complains of knee pain rather than hip pain. Which nerv

params.question: A child with hip disease complains of knee pain rather than hip pain. Which nerve explains this referred pain, as described by Hilton's Law?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The femoral nerve supplies anterior thigh muscles and the knee joint, but it is not the nerve cited in this scenario. The obturator nerve supplies both the hip and knee joints, causing the referred pain.

Correct. The obturator nerve supplies both the hip and knee joints. When the hip is diseased, pain signals travel along this nerve, and the brain may interpret them as originating from the knee — a classic example of referred pain explained by Hilton's Law.
The sciatic nerve supplies the posterior thigh and leg. The obturator nerve — which innervates both the hip and knee joints — is the one that explains hip pathology presenting as knee pain.
The tibial nerve is a branch of the sciatic nerve, primarily supplying structures below the knee. The obturator nerve innervates both the hip and knee joints, explaining the referred pain.

overallFeedback.feedback: The obturator nerve supplies both the hip and knee joints. When the hip is diseased, the brain cannot always localise the signal, so the child feels knee pain instead — a textbook application of Hilton's Law.
Well done. This hip-to-knee referred pain via the obturator nerve is a classic clinical application of Hilton's Law that you will encounter in orthopaedic and neuroanatomy settings.
